Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Right Fast, averaging 135.0 kph (up to 143.2).
  • Often takes the new ball (opens in 27% of innings).
  • Typical length is 8-9m (their good length); median 7.3 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length outside off, swinging both ways, seaming away, hitting the stumps (31% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 2% of the time against right-handers.
  • Across an over he holds a fairly consistent length.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Back of Length (8 wkts, 1.9% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: 5th stump.
  • Takes 20% of wickets pitching back of a length wide outside off.
  • Beats the bat 7.0% of tracked balls (false-shot 13.5%).
  • Gets you bowled more than most — 24% of his wickets (1.3× the typical rate for this type).
  • 55% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, slip: 2nd, midwicket: short.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length on the 6th stump (19% of runs).
  • Short ball: 39% of deliveries, economy 4.1 — 13 wkts from 952 short balls.
  • Away from back of length, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak square/through the off side (45%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes back of a length wide outside off (economy 4.8, beats the bat only 9%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length outside off, swinging both ways, seaming away, hitting the stumps (31% of deliveries, economy 2.95). Minor variations: a good length wide outside off (25%) and back of a length wide outside off (10%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length outside off ▶swinging both ways, seaming away31%2.951013%
a good length wide outside off ▶seaming both ways, swinging away25%2.76616%
back of a length wide outside off ▶seaming both ways, swinging away10%4.8269%
full outside off ▶swinging both ways, seaming both ways8%4.21112%
back of a length outside off ▶seaming both ways, swinging both ways7%5.08213%
full wide outside off ▶swinging away, seaming both ways7%5.66315%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

57% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 12 balls; most runs go to the off side (45%); the drive hurts most (75 fours/sixes at 2.8 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps — pitches around wide of 6th and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the leg side (54%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length wide outside off most often (24%); drops short often (39%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched back of a length wide outside off, but strikes most often pitching on the 5th stump.

Speed & Spells

He drops ~3.7 km/h from his opening spell to later spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.

By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.

By match day — fatigue across the game.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Moderately consistent with his length (length spread ±2.2 m; more repeatable than 32% of pace bowlers); holds a steady length right through the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.
Across 36 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.
Ball in overMedian lengthShort %EconWkt %

Ball 37.7 m11%3.981.2%
Ball 47.6 m11%2.861.7%
Ball 57.6 m11%3.481.5%
Ball 67.7 m11%3.591.8%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 78% of right-arm pace); releases wide of the stumps (76 cm out, wider than 71% of right-arm pace); varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 94% of right-arm pace).

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
